meta.theme: attention as the site of moral work that precedes choice

source.usage_note: Original composition in CAT register for CAT OS. The idea that moral attention precedes choice belongs to a recognisable tradition in moral philosophy (associated with Iris Murdoch), but no specific text is adapted. Personal study only.

meta.prompt_version: in-repo authored (Premium Reading Library milestone); RC_MASTER_GENERATION_PROMPT.v1 + CAT_VARC_BIBLE craft rules applied
meta.reviewer_notes: Reported-view opening with a late, deliberately qualified thesis; the main-idea near-tie is between the correct modest claim and its inflated twin. Owner review pending.

paragraphs.text: Ask someone to point to the moral moments of a life and they will usually point to decisions. The refused bribe, the difficult confession, the resignation offered on principle: morality, in this picture, is a sequence of forks, and a person's character is the sum of the branches taken. The picture has an administrative tidiness. It suggests that a moral life could be audited — that we could, in principle, list the choices, weigh each one, and arrive at a verdict. Most ethical writing, concerned as it is with what one ought to do, quietly accepts the frame: it treats the moment of decision as the place where morality happens.

A quieter tradition in moral philosophy has long suspected that this picture arrives too late. By the time a choice presents itself, most of the moral work has already been done — done in the long, unglamorous interval during which we were looking at the world and describing it to ourselves. Consider a woman who has gradually come to see an acquaintance as tiresome: needy where she might have seen loyal, theatrical where she might have seen wounded. The description feels like observation, but it is closer to authorship. When the acquaintance next asks something of her, the choice of how to respond will be made inside a scene her attention has already furnished. What looked like a fork was, in large part, the last step of a path.

The obvious objection is that attention seems the wrong sort of thing to carry moral weight. We praise and blame acts because acts are willed; noticing, by contrast, appears simply to happen to us. But this underestimates how directable attention is. What we notice is trained — by the company we keep, the descriptions we rehearse, the examples we return to — and the training is, over time, ours to conduct. A person can practise attending to what is actually there rather than to what flatters or reassures; the practice is effortful in the way any discipline is effortful; and sustained effort of that kind is precisely what we hold people responsible for. The gaze, on this view, is not passive. It is the slowest of our actions.

None of this makes decision irrelevant, and the tradition at its best does not claim so; emergencies are real, and some forks arrive unannounced. The claim is smaller and more unsettling: the dramatic choice is rarer than our moral bookkeeping assumes, and much of what we call choosing is the ratification of seeing. If that is right, the moral life is less like a courtroom, which convenes for verdicts, than like a garden, which is made or neglected continuously. The person who waits for the great decision to display her character may find, when it arrives, that her attention has already spent the inheritance.

questions.stem: Which of the following best captures the passage's central claim?

options.A: Since attention settles everything in advance, the experience of choosing is an illusion and deliberation changes nothing
options.B: Much of the moral work we attribute to moments of decision is done earlier, by the quality of attention with which we come to see the world
options.C: People routinely turn acquaintances into caricatures through uncharitable description, and owe them a juster way of seeing
options.D: A person's character is best assessed by auditing the significant decisions of their life, because decisions are where morality happens

explanation.correct_reasoning: The passage's arc is: the decision-centred picture (p1), the counter-tradition that attention does the earlier work (p2), the defence of attention as trainable and creditable (p3), and the explicitly limited conclusion (p4: 'much of what we call choosing is the ratification of seeing'). Option B states exactly this — at the passage's own strength, 'much of', not 'all of'.
explanation.question_type_note: A main-idea answer must carry the author's own position at its stated strength — not the opening view, not the example, and not an inflated version of the thesis.
explanation.reading_habit: Before judging options, write the author's claim in one sentence with its qualifier intact ('much of', 'largely', 'often') — the strongest distractor is usually your sentence with the qualifier deleted.

distractors.why_wrong: The tiresome acquaintance is one illustration inside paragraph 2; the passage's claim is about where moral work happens, not a lesson about describing people kindly.
distractors.seductive_element: The example is the most vivid and memorable thing in the passage, so it feels like the point.

distractors.why_wrong: Paragraph 4 explicitly declines this strength: 'None of this makes decision irrelevant.' The author claims choice is rarer and later than assumed — never that it is illusory.
distractors.seductive_element: It reads like the bold conclusion the argument seems to be building toward, and bold conclusions feel like main ideas.

distractors.why_wrong: This is the opening picture the passage spends three paragraphs undoing — the view that 'arrives too late'. The author reports it; the author does not hold it.
distractors.seductive_element: Paragraph 1 states this view fluently and at length, and nothing yet signals disagreement — a reader who anchored early carries it out of the passage.

questions.stem: It can most reasonably be inferred that the author would describe a person who has never practised attending justly as someone who

options.A: is more likely than others to defer to moral authorities in an emergency
options.B: tends to face choices already shaped by descriptions they never examined
options.C: will be incapable of acting well when a difficult choice finally arrives
options.D: bears no responsibility for how they see, since noticing merely happens to us

explanation.correct_reasoning: Paragraph 2 establishes that unexamined description 'furnishes' the scene of later choice, and paragraph 3 makes attention a practice one can neglect. A person who never practises just attention is therefore left with choices pre-shaped by descriptions they did not inspect — one careful step from the text, and no further. Note the hedge 'tends to': it matches the passage's own strength.
explanation.question_type_note: A correct inference is forced by the passage in one step; options that predict outright incapacity or import new scenarios go beyond what the text licenses.
explanation.reading_habit: Test every inference option against the question 'could the passage be true and this option false?' — if yes, the option is not forced, and CAT inference rewards only what is forced.

distractors.why_wrong: 'Incapable' predicts total failure; the passage says the work will be harder and largely pre-done, not that good action becomes impossible. Emergencies and real forks remain (p4).
distractors.seductive_element: It sounds like the practical cash value of the argument — neglect attention, fail the test — a satisfying moral to draw.

distractors.why_wrong: Deference to moral authorities is never mentioned or implied anywhere in the passage; nothing connects unpractised attention to reliance on others.
distractors.seductive_element: It is plausible worldly psychology, and the passage's talk of training and discipline can evoke teachers and authorities.

distractors.why_wrong: This restates the objection of paragraph 3 ('noticing appears simply to happen to us') as if the author endorsed it — but the paragraph exists to reject exactly this, arguing attention is trained and creditable.
distractors.seductive_element: It reuses the passage's own words; a reader who remembers the sentence but not its role in the argument recognises it as 'something the passage said'.

questions.stem: The author introduces the woman who has come to see an acquaintance as tiresome primarily in order to

options.A: concede the strongest objection to the quieter tradition before answering it
options.B: demonstrate that attention, unlike action, cannot be brought under deliberate control
options.C: illustrate that our private judgments of other people are frequently unkind and inaccurate
options.D: show how a settled description quietly does the work we later attribute to choice

explanation.correct_reasoning: The example sits inside paragraph 2's claim that moral work precedes choice: her description ('closer to authorship') builds the scene in which the later choice 'will be made'. Its function is to make visible how seeing pre-empts choosing — the paragraph's exact claim, carried by one concrete case.
explanation.question_type_note: Purpose questions ask what claim an example was hired to carry — the sentence it sits beside — not other true things the example happens to show.
explanation.reading_habit: When you meet an example, silently attach it to the claim it serves before reading on; purpose questions then become a matter of reading off the label you already wrote.

distractors.why_wrong: Her description is indeed uncharitable, but the paragraph is not teaching kindness in judgment; it is showing that description precedes and shapes choice. The observation is true of the example and silent about its function.
distractors.seductive_element: It matches the example's surface content perfectly, so it feels like a faithful reading.

distractors.why_wrong: The concession-and-reply happens in paragraph 3, and the objection conceded there concerns attention's passivity — not this example, which belongs to the positive case of paragraph 2.
distractors.seductive_element: The passage does contain exactly this move, so the option rings true to anyone recalling the passage's shape loosely.

distractors.why_wrong: Paragraph 3 argues precisely the reverse: attention is directable and trainable. The example shows attention's power, not its uncontrollability.
distractors.seductive_element: The woman never chose her description consciously, which can look like evidence that attention runs on its own.

questions.stem: As used in the final paragraph, "ratification" most nearly means

options.A: the postponement of a judgment that remains difficult
options.B: the formal confirmation of something already effectively settled
options.C: the correction of an earlier misjudgment
options.D: the public celebration of an achievement

explanation.correct_reasoning: The sentence — 'much of what we call choosing is the ratification of seeing' — compresses the passage's whole argument: seeing settles the matter first, and the later 'choice' merely makes it official. 'Ratification' (as when a treaty, already negotiated, is formally approved) carries exactly this sense of confirming what is effectively decided.
explanation.question_type_note: Vocabulary-in-context questions reward the meaning this sentence requires, which here must echo the argument the word is summarising.
explanation.reading_habit: Solve contextual vocabulary by replacing the word with each option and rereading the sentence inside the paragraph's argument — the right option keeps the argument intact; the others quietly change it.

distractors.why_wrong: Ratification is formal approval, not festivity; nothing in the sentence involves celebrating, and 'celebration of an achievement' breaks the sentence's logic of choice merely confirming sight.
distractors.seductive_element: Both words share an air of official occasion — treaties are ratified and then celebrated.

distractors.why_wrong: The sentence says choice confirms what seeing has done; 'correction' would have choice overturn seeing — the reverse of the passage's claim.
distractors.seductive_element: A reader who expects the essay to end by restoring choice's authority hears 'choice fixes what seeing got wrong'.

distractors.why_wrong: Nothing in the sentence or passage involves delay; ratification completes a process rather than deferring it.
distractors.seductive_element: The passage's talk of choices 'arriving' late can suggest a theme of postponement.

vocabulary.passage_use: It suggests that a moral life could be audited
vocabulary.meaning_here: formally inspected and tallied, as accounts are

vocabulary.passage_use: a scene her attention has already furnished
vocabulary.meaning_here: supplied and fitted out in advance

vocabulary.passage_use: much of what we call choosing is the ratification of seeing
vocabulary.meaning_here: formal confirmation of what is already effectively settled

mentor.challenge: This passage quietly replaces a picture you probably hold — that morality lives in big decisions. Your test is to notice when the author's own view finally arrives, and how far it actually goes.
mentor.one_sentence_summary: Most of the moral work we credit to moments of choice has already been done earlier, by the quality of attention with which we came to see the situation.
mentor.main_idea: By the time we face a moral choice, our habits of attention have largely shaped what we are able to see and do. The real moral effort is therefore continuous and earlier — though the author is careful to say decisions still matter.
mentor.simple_explanation:
We usually think of a moral life as a series of big decisions — refuse the bribe, tell the truth, resign on principle. Add up the decisions and you know the person. The author describes this familiar picture first, almost approvingly.

Then comes the turn. A quieter tradition says: by the time the big moment arrives, most of the work is already done. While you were describing people to yourself — 'she is tiresome', 'he is dramatic' — you were building the stage on which your future choice would be performed. The woman in the example never decides to be unkind; her long-settled description decides for her.

An objection is raised and answered: surely you can't be blamed for what you notice — noticing just happens. No, says the author. What you notice is trained, the way a muscle is trained, by the company you keep and the descriptions you rehearse. Slow, effortful training is exactly the kind of thing we hold people responsible for.

The ending is deliberately careful. Choices still exist; emergencies are real. The claim is only that the dramatic fork is rarer than we think, and that much of what we call choosing simply makes official what our attention already settled.
mentor.why_difficult: The passage never announces its thesis. It opens with a view it will spend three paragraphs undoing, hides its central claim inside an example about an unnamed woman, and then takes back a little of its own boldness in the last paragraph. Readers who miss that final qualification turn a careful claim ('much of the work') into an absolute one ('choice is an illusion') — and the options are built to catch exactly that slip.
mentor.author_intention: To loosen the reader's grip on a familiar picture of morality — not by attacking it head-on, but by showing that it starts the film at the last scene.

paragraph_journey.note: Morality as a list of big decisions you could audit like accounts. The author describes this view fluently — but describing is not endorsing, and no endorsement ever comes.
By the time you choose, your attention has already furnished the scene. The woman who settled on 'tiresome' made tomorrow's choice today, without noticing she was choosing anything.
Doesn't noticing just happen to us? No — attention is trainable, training takes sustained effort, and sustained effort is exactly what we hold people responsible for.
Decisions still matter; the claim is smaller. Most choosing ratifies earlier seeing — so the moral life is a garden tended continuously, not a courtroom that convenes for verdicts.

mentor.tone_progression: Even and descriptive at the start, gently subversive through the middle, and quietly firm — never triumphant — at the close. The author corrects a picture without raising their voice.
key_transitions: 'A quieter tradition in moral philosophy has long suspected that this picture arrives too late.' (start of paragraph 2) — the passage turns from reporting the common view to undoing it.
'None of this makes decision irrelevant…' (start of paragraph 4) — the author reins the claim in; the exact strength of the thesis is fixed here, and the questions test whether you noticed.
mentor.misunderstanding: Hurried readers make one of two mirror errors. Some anchor to paragraph 1 and carry away the decision-auditing picture — the very view the author dismantles. Others over-correct and remember the passage as declaring choice an illusion — a strength of claim the final paragraph explicitly declines. The passage lives between those two readings, and so do its questions.
mentor.traps_summary: Every trap here plays with the strength and ownership of claims: the opening view offered as if the author held it, the modest 'much of the work' inflated into 'all of it', and the vivid example mistaken for the thesis. Before accepting any option, ask two questions — whose view is this, and at what strength?
mentor.reading_lesson: When a passage opens with a confident, familiar picture, hold it loosely: argumentative prose usually opens with the view it intends to complicate. Mark the sentence where the author's own voice enters — signalled by words like 'suspected', 'but', 'in fact' — and let that later position, at its stated strength, be what you carry out of the passage.
mentor.real_world: Every appraisal, hiring call, and family quarrel runs on descriptions settled long before the 'decision'. Notice the words you habitually use for a colleague or a subject you dislike — by the time you must decide something about them, those words will already have voted.
mentor.takeaway: Most choices are the last step of a path your attention has already walked.
mentor.reflection_question: Which description of a person have you repeated so often that it now chooses for you?
